threat_model: PLang is user-sovereign software. The user owns their .pr files and runs them in a trusted environment. The trust boundary is cryptographic signatures on Data — once verified, everything inside runs with full trust. Security focuses on: (1) DoS at transport boundaries where untrusted data enters, (2) integrity of the signature/verification mechanism, (3) robustness against malformed external input.

attack_surface.exposure: UnwrapJsonElement (Data.cs:208-278), JsonStringNavigator (JsonStringNavigator.cs:20-37), fromJson action (fromJson.cs:26-41) — all recursively unwrap JSON without depth limits
attack_surface.trust_boundary: External data entering via transport channels, API responses, file reads from untrusted sources, decompressed envelope payloads
mitigations: System.Text.Json default MaxDepth=64 on JsonDocument.Parse (not on UnwrapJsonElement recursion)
gaps: No depth limit on UnwrapJsonElement recursion
No size limit on JSON string parsing in JsonStringNavigator
Duplicate UnwrapJsonElement in fromJson.cs (code duplication = inconsistent fixes)

attack_surface.trust_boundary: Decompress processes untrusted compressed+serialized Data from external channels/transport. This is the PRIMARY trust boundary.
gaps: RehydrateNestedData has no recursion depth limit
Verified is a public settable bool — data can claim 'verified' without cryptographic proof

attack_surface.exposure: Data.GetChild (Data.Navigation.cs:14-65), ValueNavigators dispatch chain, ListNavigator implicit first delegation (ListNavigator.cs:39-41)
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Navigation paths derived from external data (e.g., navigating into decompressed/deserialized structures from transport)
gaps: No recursion depth limit on GetChild
ListNavigator implicit first delegation can chain recursively

attack_surface.trust_boundary: Type names from external sources (e.g., type field in deserialized Data from transport)
mitigations: Known type names are finite in _nameToClr
gaps: Clr() recursion on nested generics has no depth limit

findings.vector: UnwrapJsonElement (Data.cs:208-278) recursively unwraps JsonElement objects/arrays without any depth counter. A JSON document with deeply nested structures causes StackOverflowException. The same pattern exists in fromJson.cs:26-41 (duplicate code).
findings.preconditions: Untrusted JSON enters via transport channel, API response, or external file. System.Text.Json default MaxDepth=64 limits initial parse, but UnwrapJsonElement's own recursion has no limit.
findings.impact: Process crash (StackOverflowException cannot be caught). Denial of service.
findings.exploit_sketch: Send deeply nested JSON through transport channel. Data constructor calls UnwrapJsonElement which recurses once per nesting level. Also exploitable via fromJson action on untrusted input.
findings.proposed_fix: Add depth parameter to UnwrapJsonElement, UnwrapJsonObject, UnwrapJsonArray. Throw InvalidDataException when depth exceeds 128. Share implementation between Data.cs and fromJson.cs to eliminate duplicate.

findings.vector: RehydrateNestedData (Data.Envelope.cs:184-200) recursively reconstructs Data objects from deserialized dictionaries without depth limit. This sits at the transport boundary — exactly where untrusted data arrives via Decompress.
findings.preconditions: Attacker sends compressed Data payload through transport channel. Payload decompresses to valid JSON with deeply nested {name,value} dictionary structures. Highly compressible (~2KB compressed → 5000 levels of nesting).
findings.impact: Process crash (StackOverflowException). Denial of service at the transport boundary.
findings.exploit_sketch: Craft JSON: {"name":"a","value":{"name":"b","value":...}} nested 5000 levels. GZip compress (small payload). Send through channel. Decompress → deserialize → RehydrateNestedData recurses until stack overflow.
findings.proposed_fix: Add depth parameter: RehydrateNestedData(Data data, int depth = 0). Check depth > 100, throw InvalidDataException.

findings.vector: Data.GetChild (Data.Navigation.cs:14-65) recursively parses dot/bracket paths without depth limit. Relevant when navigating into structures received from untrusted sources (e.g., decompressed transport data).
findings.preconditions: Path with thousands of segments navigating into deeply nested external data. Combined with ListNavigator implicit first delegation, shorter paths can amplify through nested lists.
findings.exploit_sketch: Deeply nested external data structure + navigation path. GetChild recurses at line 64 for each segment.
findings.proposed_fix: Add depth parameter to GetChild. Max 100 segments. Or convert to iterative path parsing.

findings.vector: MemoryStack.Set() accepts writes to !-prefixed system variables.
findings.preconditions: User writes set %!fileSystem% = null in their own code.
findings.impact: N/A — user-sovereign action. PLang's security model: the user owns their software.
findings.proposed_fix: No fix needed. This is equivalent to Environment.Exit() in C# — the user's prerogative.

findings.vector: ObjectNavigator exposes all public properties of objects stored in Data.Value via reflection, including Engine, FileSystem, Context.
findings.preconditions: User navigates to system objects via %!engine.fileSystem% etc.
findings.impact: N/A — intentional capability. .pr files run in trusted environment.
findings.proposed_fix: No fix needed. User access to their own engine internals is by design.

findings.vector: JsonStringNavigator (JsonStringNavigator.cs:20-37) parses any string starting with { or [ as JSON with no size limit. Re-parses on every navigation access (no caching).
findings.preconditions: Large JSON string from external source stored in variable, then navigated.
findings.impact: Memory exhaustion. Amplified by repeated access.
findings.exploit_sketch: External API returns 100MB JSON string. User code navigates into it. JsonDocument.Parse loads entire string, UnwrapElement doubles memory usage.
findings.proposed_fix: Add size limit before parsing (e.g., 10MB). Consider caching parsed results.

findings.vector: MemoryStack.ResolveVariablesInPath (MemoryStack.cs:207-215) recursively resolves bracket variables. Circular references cause stack overflow.
findings.preconditions: Two variables reference each other in bracket notation. Can happen with external data that sets variable values.
findings.exploit_sketch: Variable 'idx' = 'items[idx2]', variable 'idx2' = 'items[idx]'. Access %items[idx]% → infinite resolution loop.
findings.proposed_fix: Add recursion depth counter or HashSet cycle detection to ResolveVariablesInPath.

findings.vector: Newtonsoft compatibility shim (Data.cs:228-258) detects JToken types by namespace string only, without verifying assembly identity.
findings.preconditions: Requires library.load to load a custom assembly — which already grants code execution, making this vector redundant.
findings.impact: Low practical risk. If library.load gives RCE, namespace spoofing adds nothing.
findings.exploit_sketch: Custom assembly in Newtonsoft.Json.Linq namespace with weaponized Value property getter. Requires library.load first.
findings.proposed_fix: Harden by checking assembly name is 'Newtonsoft.Json'. Non-blocking.

findings.vector: Verified (Data.Envelope.cs:42) is a public settable bool? with no cryptographic backing. Any code can set Verified = true, claiming data is cryptographically verified when it is not. Signatures are PLang's trust boundary — once data is 'verified', everything inside runs with full trust. A settable bool undermines this entire model.
findings.preconditions: Any code path sets data.Verified = true without performing actual signature validation. Or serialized Data contains Verified=true (though currently [JsonIgnore]).
findings.impact: Trust boundary bypass. Data claims verified status without cryptographic proof. Since verified data runs with full trust, this is a direct path to privilege escalation once the signature system is implemented.
findings.exploit_sketch: data.Verified = true; — now any code checking if (data.Verified == true) treats this as cryptographically validated. The entire 'signatures are the wall' model collapses if the wall has a door anyone can open.
findings.proposed_fix: Make Verified { get; private set; } or better: compute it from Signature. Add a VerifySignature() method that does actual crypto and sets Verified as the output. No external code should be able to claim 'verified' without providing a valid signature.

findings.vector: Clr() method (Types/this.cs:407-454) recursively parses generic type syntax without depth limit.
findings.preconditions: Deeply nested type name from external source (e.g., type field in deserialized Data from transport).
findings.exploit_sketch: Type name: list<list<list<...>>> nested 5000 levels. Clr() recurses at lines 416 and 429-430.
findings.proposed_fix: Add depth parameter to Clr(). Max 20 levels.

findings.preconditions: Code path that merges Data in a loop.
findings.exploit_sketch: Event handler merging results from millions of steps.
findings.proposed_fix: Add max size check on merged list.

findings.exploit_sketch: Deep nested Data + debug mode → clone exhausts memory.
findings.proposed_fix: Consider shallow clone or size limits.

summary: v2 — Updated after creator review. PLang's security model is user-sovereign: .pr files run in trusted environment, user actions are not attacks. The real trust boundary is cryptographic signatures on Data. Findings #4 (system variable writes) and #5 (ObjectNavigator reflection) reclassified as by-design. Finding #9 (Verified settable bool) upgraded to HIGH — signatures are the wall, and a public setter lets anyone claim 'verified' without crypto. This directly undermines the trust model. Finding #8 (Newtonsoft shim) downgraded to LOW — library.load already gives RCE, namespace spoofing adds nothing. Recursion findings (#1-3, #7, #10) remain real DoS vectors at transport boundaries. Priority: fix #9 (Verified integrity), then #1-3 (recursion at transport boundary), then #7/#10 (recursion elsewhere).
